A. Appendix

A1. Miscellaneous Facts

  • In the normal model , the sample variance satisfies

    (97)

    For large and any distribution, the asymptotics are

    (98)

    where for a normal distribution.

  • is efficient in the normal model. Thus is not an efficient estimator for .

  • In the normal model, the mean is BLUE for predicting This is easy to prove.

    • If the distribution is laplace, the sample median has smaller variance than the mean.
  • The least absolute deviations (LAD) estimate arises as the MLE if the errors have a Laplace distribution.
  • In general for convex , and cannot simultaneously be unbiased by Jensen’s inequality, and hence both cannot be efficient.
  • The sample mean is both the MLE and unique minimum variance unbiased risk estimator (MVUE) for estimating the mean from samples of a multivariate Gaussian with dimension less than 3. It is asymptotically efficient. For dimension , the James-Stein estimator dominates the mean (least squares).

  • Approximation Theorems

    • Binomial with large small -> Poisson
    • Binomial with large and fixed -> Gaussian
    • Poisson with -> Gaussian
  • Use Cholesky decomposition and change of coordinates to derive the pdf of and sample a multivariate normal distribution. We are applying a change of basis to the covariance function, which is a bilinear map.

  • Proof that a random walk comes back to origin almost surely in one dimension

    • Sum over catalan numbers using Sterling’s approximation and use Borel cantelli converse lemma (jk this is wrong since the events are not independent.)
    • You can also just use OST with two walls and take the limit as one hits infinity.
  • Minimax estimators are defined to have lowest maximum risk among all estimators. The maximum is over the possible values of the true parameter.
  • Gaussian mixture models: Expectation–Maximization (EM): alternate between soft-assigning points to clusters (E-step) and re-estimating parameters (M-step).

  • Regressing for the mean / horizontal line is a special case of linear regression. I.e. we can get z-intervals from the sampling distribution for if we set . For GLS, we obtain the precision-weighted mean instead of just the mean.

    • We can even estimate the mean of a Gaussian from noisy measurments. We just add two independent covariance matrices to our data, as for independent vectors we add their covariance matrices.
  • Subspaces admit multiple projections and one unique orthogonal projection if the inner product is defined.
  • Bias–variance is the classical tradeoff for estimators under the assumed model. Robustness extends this picture: the mean is efficient but fragile, while the median is less efficient but robust. In practice we balance all three depending on whether we trust the model assumptions or expect contamination.
  • Gradient descent = first-order, cheap, slow. Newton’s method = second-order, expensive, but much faster near the solution.

  • [REVIEW] Law of total variance:

    (99)

  • Computing matrix derivatives: use definition of derivative for quick answer or directional derivatve (more rigorous): Directional derivative of f(x) dot h = derivatev of f(x+th) wrt t at t=0.

A1.1. Data Science

  • You run a backtest with hundreds of candidate features and find 5 with very high Sharpe. Why should you be suspicious, and how do you correct for it?

    • If you have one sample of historical data, then by chance you may just get a high sharpe ratio has its own sampling distribution. This is known as data snooping (noise is sometimes crazy).
    • We should use out-of-sample validation.
    • Use bootstrapping to get sampling distribution of Sharpe (distribution of sample).
    • Estimate confidence intervals for Sharpe (distribution of generalization error)
    • Bonferroni correction: Say we run t-tests for each strategy for sharpe > 0. We should set our alpha to instead of just , that way we account for the family-wise error rate.

    • A similar effect is called p-value hacking, where you manipulate the data analysis enough times to get a significant p-value.

      • E.g. apply transformation. Remove an outlier, etc. Each time we reroll the null hypothesis type I error chance. With 20 independent tests and , we have chance to p-hack. The tests from tweaking obviously aren’t independent but we don’t know whether it’s close to or . This also breaks the logic of classical hypothesis testing. You saw the data already and did stuff.
      • Solution is to use out-of-sample validation.

  • Dangers of lags/autoregressive: Multicollinearity explosion, overfitting, data-snooping

    • [TODO] Use ARIMA

  • Checking redundacy of 2 parameters

    • Check correlation between A and B
    • Try with A and without B and vice versa with cross validation
    • Fit residulas using A and y to B
    • Two parameters may be correlated because of seasonal variations they share (in that case the season should be a parameter, not this unrelated one. see example below). Divide it out first.
    • In general, we can assess multicollinearity (seeing if a specific parameter is redudant), be rgeressing all the parameters on it and checking . Define the variance inflation factor for the jth parameter as .
  • Is validation of 51% significant? Suppose you’re measuring hit rate of a model: how often it correctly predicts up/down returns. Each prediction either succeeds (1) or fails (0). Then we can compute errorbar assumign Bernoulli and correct for dependecne.
  • Categorical variables: do one hot encoding and can proceed with OLS as normal

  • “You train a model on historical equity returns, and it shows an R² of 20% in sample, but only 1% out of sample. How would you tell if that 1% is still meaningful?”

    • Do some bootstrapping to get a distribution for . Then can compute CI or hypothesis test
    • Can do cross validation as well: check for persistent across folds.
  • Gaussian: and . Formulas are on https://chatgpt.com/c/68b3a5d6-da10-8327-a7b4-b4c67c14438a

  • Spearman correlation is the Pearson correlation between and (rank is literally analogous to argsort, with decimal places for ties.) Pearson detects linearity and Spearman detects monotonicity. Spearman is less sensitive to outliers.

    • The probability of obtaining cumulative sum when rolling die is . Is there an intuitive explanation for this without invoking renewal theory? Also for between 1 and 6 we have . See https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/5099407

A2. Multivariate Taylor Series

(100)

The th order term when “contracted” (generalized dot product) with one copy of yields an th tensor, and we proceed recursively. Think of taking numpy dot along an axis.

Proof

Define the function and apply the ordinary single-variable Taylor Theorem. We even get the Lagrange error bound automatically: the remainder is the th term where the derivative is evaluated somewhere on the line connecting and .

□

A3. Facts about the Gaussian

A3.1. Why is there a 1/2 in the Gaussian?

We could have just defined the Gaussian as

(101)

so that and . But then the variance takes the ugly form instead of simply . We can fix things by setting , recovering the familiar form.

A3.2. Abundance of the Gaussian

Why do heights, measurement errors, exam scores seem to follow a normal distribution? Why do income, weight, investment returns follow a log-normal distribution? Apply Lindeberg-Feller CLT.

  • Height is sum of genetic contributions, environmental influences, development randomness. Empirically and biologically these effects are additive (and not multiplicative), so we get a normal distribution instead of a log normal distribution.

  • Measurement error is usually the sum of many small, independent effects: e.g. error in measuring the length of a rod is affected by

    • thermal noise in the sensor
    • electronic shot noise,
    • slight hand jitter in positioning
    • rounding or quantization error
    • ambient vibrations

Earthquake magnitudes, financial crashes, and city sizes are driven not by aggregation but rare shocks so they are heavy-tailed.

These are used to derive - and -intervals

A4. Poisson distribution

Suppose we observe on average independent events in a time , that is on average the probability of observing an event in a small time interval is . Then, the probability of observing molecules in a time is

(102)

Use a recursion like argument to derive. [https://www.pp.rhul.ac.uk/~cowan/stat/notes/PoissonNote.pdf] You can also derive it with the binomial distribution.

Similiar setup: consider a small volume of a much larger volume of gas. Suppose the chance for an individual molecule to be found in is exceedingly small, but the average number of molecules in is known to be . Then the probability of finding precisely molecules in is given by the Poisson distribution,

(103)

It turns out the . Is there an intuitive reason for why this should be the case?

Fun fact, the chance of getting even number from Poisson distribution approaches 0.5 as . Relevant for crossing over in biology.

Poisson to exponential is trivial (just evaluate pmf at ). [TODO] The other way around seems annoying.

A5. Convex Optimization

  • Convex functions have global minimum (geometrically obvious). Local extrema implies global minimum so we can just check for stationary points.

  • A smooth function is convex if its hessian is positive semidefinite everywhere. For a quadratic form

    (104)

    the hessian is simply . We have a unique global minimum if is positive definite.

    • If the function is convex but not smooth, e.g. , then we use subgradients to check for convexity. The set of subgradients as a point is the set of hyperplanes that lie under the function.
  • Constrained optimization: use lagrange multipliers for equality constraints.

  • If we have inequalties, use Karush–Kuhn–Tucker conditions KKT:

    Suppose we want to minimize subject to

    (105)

    Then is optimal (under convexity + some regularity assumptions) if there exists such that

    1. Stationarity

      (106)

    2. Primal feasibility

      (107)

    3. Dual feasibility

      (108)

    4. Complementary slackness

      (109)

    If , then the inequality constraint does nothing and we have . If , then can be positive, preventing the optimizer from leaving the feasible region (draw a picture).

Cyclic/random coordinate descent is a competitor with gradient descent. Trade per iteration progress with per iteration cost.

A6. Nonconvex Optimization

Simulated annealing is a metaheuristic inspired by metallurgy: when you cool a metal slowly, atoms settle into a low-energy crystalline state; if you cool too quickly, you get defects.

Example 17. Pick weights to maximize Sharpe ratio, but you have nonlinear cost terms and cardinality constraints (only up to 10 different stocks).

(110)

For example let the cost be the slippage penalty

(111)

The cardinality constraint makes the problem NP hard, like a knapsack problem. The subject is called “Integer programming”. “With cardinality constraints the problem is no longer convex, so you either use a mixed-integer solver or approximate by heuristics like greedy selection or continuous relaxations”

Other examples where nonconvex learning appear are:

  • Fitting a complicated stochastic model to observed data
  • Picking a strategy (market making, momentum, arbitage) at different discrete times and optimize with switching costs (finite-horizon control problem, also called bandit problem since no state transitions)
  • ML for alpha

A7. Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

  • Covariance is symmetric and PSD, implying that it is diagonalizable with nonnegative eigenvalues.
  • Diagonalize covariance matrix and reexpress data with the orthonormal eigenbasis. That way you have uncorrelated variables. E.g. if you have two RVs, physics and math test scores. A PCA might decompose into the sum of the scores over (overall ability) and the difference of the scores over (subject tilt).
  • It is easier to work in the basis where the variables are uncorrelated (for a Gaussian this implies independence).

  • PCA only cares about variance along straight-line directions in the input space. If the interesting structure is nonlinear (spirals, circles, clusters), PCA won’t capture it. E.g., let and be distributed according to a circle. PCA won’t tell you to get rid of anything.

    • To capture nonlinear structure, we use kernal PCA, t-SNE, or autoencoders.

Example 18 (Reducing multicollinearity in linear regression). Say we regress house price on

We might expect big variance along their sum. Small variance among , and small variance along .

In practice to stabilize against multicollinearity, we can do PCR regression, where we keep only the top principal components. This is further justified by random matrix theory.

[TODO] PCA is the best linear compression scheme under squared-error loss.

A8. Decision Theory and Saint Petersburg Paradox

  • Saint Petersburg’s Paradox: People maximize expected utility, not expected dollar payoff. With diminishing marginal utility (e.g. , by Jensens), the expected utility is finite, and the answer is like up to 4 dollars with no initial wealth (set ). With inital wealth , you want to compare to .
  • See also: Super Saint Peteburg Paradox.
  • See also Von Neumann–Morgenstern utility theorem: rational choice under uncertainty involves making decisions that take the form of maximizing the expected value of some cardinal utility function.
  • A risk-neutral agent values uncertain outcomes purely by their expected value. They don’t demand extra compensation for variability (unlike risk-averse agents) and they don’t seek out variance (unlike risk-seeking agents). If offered: 50% chance of $100 and 50% chance of $0, versus a guaranteed $50, a risk-neutral person is indifferent between them, because both have the same expected payoff of $50.‘

A9. Game Theory

  • Strategies for a one-shot game

    • Pure strategy: player picks one action with certainty
    • Mixed strategy: player picks random action according to distribution.

  • Nash equilibrium is a situation where no player could gain more by changing their own strategy.

    • For example, for an entry game, the Nash equilibria are (Enter, StayOut), (Stayout, Enter), and (Enter with p, Enter with p). (Enter with p, Stayout) is not a NE because first person to change to (Enter, Stayout).
    • Generally NEs take the form of pure strategies or mixed strategies

Definition 17 (Dominance).

  1. Strategy is strictly dominated by strategy if for every possible strategy profile of the other players ,

    (112)

No rational player should ever play a strictly dominated strategy, like guessing over in 2/3-of-the-average.

  1. Strategy is weakly dominated by strategy if for every possible strategy profile of the other players ,

    (113)

    .

  2. Strategy is a dominant strategy if it strictly dominates all other strategies for player . That is, it’s the best action regardless of what others do.

For mixed strategies we use expected payoff.

Example 19 (Matching Pennies). Two players, A and B. Each round they simultaneously choose H or T.

  • If match → A gets +1, B gets −1.
  • If mismatch → A gets −1, B gets +1.

If only playing a single round, there is no pure strategy Nash equilibrium. The mixed equilibrium is to play or with prob 1/2 and 1/2.

Example 20 (2/3 of the average game). players choose numbers in an interval, usually [0,100]. Winner is the one closest to 2/3×(average guess). Ties split. Payoffs don’t change the logic; assume “win = 1, else 0”.

The unique Nash equilibrium (NE) is everyone chooses 0. No strictly dominated strategy exists. Many strategies are strictly dominated by lower numbers.

  • Classical game theory assumes unbounded rationality, but we account for bounded rationality, noisiness, and slowness by introducing known biases, noisy traders, and RL-based agents (that learn slowly and noisily).

  2. 2You can derive this with indicator variables
  3. 3(Footnote spans two pages). When has full column rank, this equals its Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse. More generally the pseudoinverse of is the inverse (restricted to ) of the orthogonal projection onto W, which minimizes distances. Enforcing uniqueness is nice and all from a theoretical perspective but it breaks down the interpretation of in statistics. is still unique (just orthogonal projection). I.e. multicollinearity is fine for in-sample prediction but not inference or out-of-sample prediction (see Section 3.1.1). We can check that MP pseudoinverse is made nicely with SVD (for intro to SVD, see 701ps12. pseudoinverse comes nicely because singular values of 0 correspond to null space of . MP chooses to project onto )
